content:
Google hired him to make Go mainstream. Six years and one generics release later, he called the mission finished — and quit.

7 min read
Just now

--

Photo by Mathias Reding on Unsplash.Forty-four thousand stars. Three thousand two hundred forks. Three hundred twenty-nine people have contributed code to it[2].
None of those numbers explain what it actually is.
What it actually is: the thing that decides what happens when you type kubectl get pods and hit enter. The thing behind docker run. The thing GitHub's own gh command line tool is built on top of.
One person started it. Then, at the point where you’d expect him to cash in the win, he quit the company that had been paying him to build it.
Every major CLI tool you use runs on the same skeleton
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Illustration by Renee French, via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0.Before Cobra existed, every Go developer building a command-line tool solved the same problems from scratch: how do you parse kubectl get pods --namespace=prod, generate --help text that doesn't lie. How…
